turns.text: Hello Clemmie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s strange to be the one sitting on this paper, honestly. Most days, I’m the one reading the data—pouring over meta-analyses and patient outcome reports for the journal. I know exactly what the "ideal" clinical trajectory looks like, but when I’m here, sitting in this clinic in Springfield, the numbers don’t comfort me. They just feel like a countdown.

I have two children already, and both times, the labor was—by the books—perfect. But my mind doesn’t stop at "perfect." I keep thinking about all the things that *could* have gone wrong, or the things I’ve read about regarding microbiome colonization or long-term immune markers. It’s hard to just be a mother when you’re constantly analyzing your own biology like it’s a failing lab experiment.

And then there’s the quiet part—the part I don't mention to the doctor. Being here as someone whose immigration status feels like a permanent, shaky footnote to my existence… it makes me feel like I have to perform being a "good" patient. I never push, I never ask the extra questions, I just smile and nod so I don’t become a statistic. I want this third time to be joyful, but right now, it mostly feels like I’m holding my breath, waiting for something to slip.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I suppose I expected a partnership—some sort of meeting of the minds. I wanted to walk in, show the provider that I’ve already optimized my folic acid intake, that I’ve done the heavy lifting on my own health data, and have them say, "Great work, let’s keep going." I wanted it to be collaborative, like peers discussing a project.

But the reality? It’s a very one-sided performance. When I’m in that room, I don't feel like a partner; I feel like a subject. The advice I get is… generic. It feels like they’re reading from a script for someone who hasn’t spent their life reading the actual trials. And because of my status, I’m terrified that if I sound too informed, or if I push back on a suggestion, I’ll be labeled "difficult." In my experience, "difficult" patients get less care, not more. 

So, I stay quiet. I nod at the basic prenatal pamphlets they hand me, even though I’ve already cited the papers those pamphlets were likely based on. It’s lonely, in a way. I’m doing all this internal preparation to ensure a healthy pregnancy, but the actual healthcare system feels like a closed door. I’m just trying to navigate it without drawing any attention to myself.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I expected to feel steady. I’m twenty-eight, I’ve raised two kids, and I work in medicine—I thought I would be the picture of calm, logical preparation. I imagined myself walking into this process with a sense of quiet authority over my own body, like I’d finally mastered the rhythm of it all. I thought the anxiety would stay locked away in my office, separate from the "joy" of growing a family.

But the reality is much heavier. My emotional state is less "prepared" and more "braced for impact." I find myself tracing the lines on my palms during appointments, my heart hammering against my ribs, just praying that the doctor doesn’t ask a question that forces me to reveal too much about my life outside these walls. 

It isn't a joy yet; it’s a constant, low-level vigilance. I’m managing my gut health, my supplements, and my stress, but it feels like I'm building a house on a cliffside. I know the foundation is solid—the data says it is—but the wind is always blowing. I wanted to feel powerful, but mostly, I just feel fragile. I’m constantly editing myself, trying to be the "perfect", invisible patient, and that takes a toll on your heart that no textbook could ever predict.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

That’s the hardest question to answer, because naming what’s missing feels like admitting that the "perfect" setup I’ve built isn't actually enough. 

What’s missing is a space where I don’t have to be the expert, and I don't have to be the immigrant. I need a space where I can be just a person. When I talk to providers, they see a "low-risk" case and check a box. They don't see the woman who stays up until 2:00 AM wondering if a single administrative hurdle in my residency status could jeopardize my access to care if something—god forbid—went wrong. I crave a professional who would look at me and say, "I know you know the data, but how are *you* holding up?" Instead, I get the clinical coldness I’ve documented in my own journals. 

My partner is wonderful, but he’s in the thick of our life, trying to keep us afloat too. He doesn't need to know the specific ways I’m terrified; it would only break his heart, and he needs to be strong for the kids. I feel like I’m carrying this invisible weight for everyone. I’m missing a witness—someone who sees the research and the humanity equally, and who acknowledges that being a person in this system shouldn't require such a constant, silent, and exhausting performance.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find the answers to my hyper-specific anxieties—the deep-dive, peer-reviewed data on how maternal lifestyle choices ripple out into neonatal immune development. I thought I’d find an intelligent community of people who were also looking at the literature, people who didn't just want "five tips for a healthy glow."

Instead, what I found was... sanitized. The apps and websites are all designed with this relentless, bubbly positivity. They’re filled with generic advice about eating leafy greens and "trusting the process." It’s deeply isolating. When I scroll through those forums, I’m surrounded by people asking about morning sickness or baby names, and I’m here wondering about the specific inflammatory markers in my system. I can’t exactly post a question about how my immigration status might color the quality of the postpartum care I receive without sounding like I’m spiraling.

None of those resources address the *reality* of being a human in a compromised position. They don’t talk about the power imbalance in the exam room or the fear of being seen as "non-compliant." I needed a roadmap for navigating the *system*, not just the biology. I needed to know how to be safe, not just how to be pregnant. What I found was a mirror of the clinical detachment I see at work—all surface, no substance, and absolutely zero help for the part of me that is scared.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The gap is vast, but it’s not really about the medical facts—it’s about the *intent*. 

Digital sources, as sanitized as they are, act like I have full agency. They offer these vast, algorithmic menus of "choices" for birth plans and prenatal care, framing everything as if I’m a consumer in a marketplace. They make it sound like I can curate my experience by just asking the right questions or requesting specific, evidence-based interventions.

But when I sit in front of my provider, that agency evaporates. There’s a quiet, rigid hierarchy. If the internet suggests I should advocate for a certain screening or a specific approach to monitoring, I know that if I actually bring that up in the clinic, I risk being branded as a "difficult" patient. In my world—the world of someone with my status—being "difficult" is a luxury I cannot afford. I’ve seen the clinical data on how bias functions; I know that being labeled "demanding" often leads to a subtle, yet measurable, withdrawal of empathy and care.

So, the gap is between the *theoretical autonomy* I’m promised by the internet and the *performative compliance* I’m forced to adopt in the exam room. The internet tells me I have a voice; my experience tells me that using that voice might be the very thing that makes me less safe.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

While I don’t have a clinical diagnosis like diabetes or hypertension, I have always viewed my immigration status as a "pre-existing condition" in the eyes of the healthcare system. Before I started this journey, I expected—or at least hoped—that a preconception visit would be a chance to build a bridge. I wanted to tell a doctor, "Here is where I am, here are my fears about how I’m viewed, and here is how we can make sure I’m safe and heard." I expected a human conversation about how to navigate the system so I wouldn’t feel like I was constantly operating in the shadows.

What actually happened was a twenty-minute appointment that barely scratched the surface. I walked in, prepared to be vulnerable, and was met with a standard checklist. "Folic acid? Check. Blood pressure? Good. You're low-risk." That was it. 

The doctor didn't ask about my life, my stressors, or the quiet fear that keeps me up at night. Because I couldn't bring myself to break my "perfect patient" performance, I just nodded and left. It’s a strange, bitter irony: I have all this knowledge, I know how to interpret the risks, and yet I feel completely un-cared for. They fixed the biology, but they left the human entirely out of the equation.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Advocate? No, it’s the exact opposite. If I advocate, I risk everything. 

In this system, advocacy is coded as "resistance" or "disruption." I’ve seen enough patient charts to know what doctors write about women who ask too many questions or challenge the status quo: *Anxious. Poor historian. Non-compliant.* If those words end up in my file, I’m not just a person anymore; I’m a liability. And for someone in my position, being a liability is the most dangerous place to be.

The system is anything but proactive. It’s a passive, gatekeeping machine. It waits for me to show up, do what I’m told, and leave without making a sound. There is no space for me to say, "I am scared that if I have a complication, my status will make me invisible to you." Instead, I proactively monitor *myself*. I am my own advocate, my own scientist, and my own protector. 

I don't look for support from the system because I don't trust it to be safe. I look for silence. I try to be the most "textbook" patient possible—polite, informed enough to agree, but never so informed that I become a threat to their routine. It’s a performance of total submission, and it is absolutely exhausting. I am guarding my own health while playing the part of a patient who doesn't need to be watched.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I pictured us as a team—two people sitting at the kitchen table, mapping out our future with the same precision I use for my research. I expected us to have these long, philosophical conversations about our values, our fears, and the exact kind of birth experience we wanted. I thought we would be partners in the research, vetting clinics and providers together, feeling this shared sense of intentionality.

The reality has been much more fragmented. My partner is supportive, of course, but he isn’t in the medical journals every day like I am. He doesn't see the systemic risks the way I do; to him, the clinic is just a building, and the doctor is just an authority figure. He doesn’t understand why I’m so hyper-vigilant about being "perfect," and honestly, I haven't been able to fully explain it to him without seeing the fear flicker in his own eyes. 

I don’t want to be the one who carries all the worry, but I find myself doing it anyway, to keep him from feeling that same cold knot of anxiety. I’ve ended up "protecting" him from the scope of my concerns, which has made me feel lonelier than I ever imagined. We aren’t really partners in the *process*; we’re partners in the *hope*, but the actual work of preparing—and all the dread that comes with it—stays tucked away in my head.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It’s not just "disproportionate"—it’s entirely mine. Every time I look at a study about prenatal nutrition or the long-term impacts of stress hormones on a fetus, I’m the one doing the math. I’m the one optimizing my gut health, tracking my cycles, and calculating the exact window for conception. 

What drives this? It’s the knowledge that I’m the one with the most to lose. My partner doesn't walk into a clinic with the same background radiation of fear that I do. He isn't the one whose immigration status could be scrutinized if a medical record gets "flagged." Because of that, I feel a crushing sense of responsibility to ensure that *nothing* goes wrong, because "wrong" for me has different, much more permanent consequences than it does for others.

I don’t delegate this labor because I don’t think anyone else *can* carry the weight of these specific anxieties. If I let my guard down, even for a moment, I feel like I’m leaving a door open to disaster. So, I keep it all on my shoulders, performing this silent, perfect, hyper-prepared motherhood before I’m even pregnant. It’s a lonely sort of labor, this constant, private policing of my own body and future.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't experienced a loss, which is, in its own way, a source of profound, quiet terror. Because my first two pregnancies were "textbook," I live in constant fear that I’ve used up my luck. I know the statistics—I know the probability of things remaining smooth forever is a curve that eventually bends. Every day I spend without a complication feels like a debt I’m accruing, and I’m terrified of when the bill will come due.

When I seek support, I expect the system to acknowledge that "low risk" is a status, not a guarantee. I want them to help me manage the *anticipatory grief* that comes with knowing too much. But the support available? It’s completely nonexistent. The system is designed to respond to *events*, not to *fears*. 

If I walked in and said, "I’m terrified that my luck is running out," they would likely just tell me my labs look fine and send me home. They don't have a protocol for the mental toll of being an "expert patient." There’s no room for a conversation about the fragility of a healthy pregnancy because, in their eyes, if nothing is wrong, there’s nothing to treat. So, I carry the expectation of a loss that hasn't happened yet, entirely on my own, while the system remains oblivious.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

I need to clarify—I haven't actually experienced a pregnancy loss, and I think that’s almost worse. Because my record is "clean," there’s nothing on paper to signal that I am someone who needs extra emotional support or a gentler touch. I’m just "low risk, multigravida." It feels like the system is waiting for me to break before it bothers to notice me. 

There’s this cold, clinical arrogance in the way they look at a "perfect" history. They assume that because I haven’t lost a pregnancy, I shouldn't be anxious, as if my body’s history is a guarantee for the future. It’s infuriating. I want someone to look at my chart and see the *person* behind the successful births, not just the successful outcomes. I want them to acknowledge that my background and my status make me feel like I’m walking a tightrope, and that the lack of past trauma doesn't mean I’m not terrified of future complications. 

Instead, they treat the *absence* of loss as an excuse to minimize my concerns. It’s like they’re waiting for me to become a problem—a statistic of a loss—before they’ll treat me like a person who needs to be heard. It makes me feel like I have to keep my "textbook" record perfect, because if I do stumble, I have no idea if they’ll even be there to catch me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that gets lost—that I really want to emphasize—is how exhausting it is to be a "high-functioning" patient. People assume that because I have the education, because I can read a journal and understand the statistics, that I am "empowered." They see me as someone who has all the tools to navigate this.

But the reality is that those tools are also what keep me awake at night. The system doesn't account for the fact that *knowing* too much is a form of trauma in itself when you feel like a ghost in the system. I have this desperate need for someone to just stop the clock, look me in the eye, and treat me like a person who is afraid, rather than a data point that is "low risk."

I’m doing this because I want to grow my family, but it feels like I’m constantly swimming against a current of indifference. I just wish there was a way for the clinical world to value the *human* as much as they value the outcome. If I could change one thing, it would be for providers to realize that the women who seem the most "together"—the ones who have done all the research and have the perfect history—are often the ones struggling the hardest to hold it all together in the dark.
